text: The sociologist Robert Putnam documented how television eroded civic participation across decades; today's researchers point to a more granular disruption. Each notification interrupt costs not merely the seconds spent reading it, but what cognitive scientists call the "resumption lag" — the four to twenty-three minutes required to fully re-engage with interrupted deep work. Multiplied across a knowledge worker's day, these micro-interruptions collectively hollow out the cognitive hours available for sustained reasoning. The problem is structural, not motivational: the architecture of attention-harvesting platforms is optimized to interrupt, not to restore.
question: According to the passage, what is 'resumption lag'?

options.text: The delay before a notification appears on screen
The time required to fully re-engage with deep work after an interruption
The fatigue that accumulates after a full day of cognitively demanding tasks
The gap between receiving a message and choosing to respond to it
